coredata.dc:title: Anthrapyrazolone functionalized Oligo(ethylene glycol)methyl ether methacrylates based fluorescent polymeric hydrogel for selective detection of Tetracycline drug
coredata.dc:creator: Sivakumar, Gomathi
Babu, Anashwara
Anandhan, Mageshwari
Nutalapati, Venkatramaiah
Maji, Samarendra
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) antibiotics are essential for treating bacterial infections; however, overuse and improper disposal place the environment in danger and promote the growth of drug-resistant microorganisms. Monitoring their impact on living organisms is vital. Polymeric-based hydrogels offer promise in antibiotic detection due to their versatility and biocompatibility, despite their uncommon use for this purpose. In this study, we have synthesized a fluorescent polymeric hydrogel, P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) utilizing oligo(ethylene glycol)methyl ether methacrylates (OEGMA) and laboratory-synthesized fluorescent anthrapyrazolone containing monomer, 2-(benzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c’d]bis(indazole)-2(7H)-yl)ethyl methacrylate (DIMA) for the fluorometric detection of TC in the aqueous medium. The P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel exhibited exceptional affinity towards TC while exhibiting high selectivity. The chemosensing behavior of the hydrogel was also compared with the DIMA monomer. Upon adding TC, emission analysis revealed a notable quenching in the emission intensity of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel as well as the DIMA monomer. The Stern Volmer rate constants (Ksv) of the hydrogel (Ksv = 9.99 x 103 M−1) in water and DIMA (Ksv = 25.63 x 103 M−1) in acetonitrile showed a higher quenching rate constant with a limit of detections (LODs) of 1.3 nM and 0.11 nM. Turn-off fluorescence behavior occurs via a static quenching mechanism through a photoinduced electron transfer process and intermolecular hydrogen bonding. To validate the selectivity, the interference analyses in an aqueous medium with different antibiotics are provided. To rationalize the real-time applicability of the hydrogel, studies were extended to water samples obtained from deionized water, tap water, lake water, and also in milk samples. The recovery rate and LOD for real samples ranged from 83.8% to 111% and 0.83 to 3.6 nM, respectively.

head.ce:title: Anthrapyrazolone functionalized Oligo(ethylene glycol)methyl ether methacrylates based fluorescent polymeric hydrogel for selective detection of Tetracycline drug

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology (SRMIST), Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology (SRMIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology (SRMIST), Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC) antibiotics are essential for treating bacterial infections; however, overuse and improper disposal place the environment in danger and promote the growth of drug-resistant microorganisms. Monitoring their impact on living organisms is vital. Polymeric-based hydrogels offer promise in antibiotic detection due to their versatility and biocompatibility, despite their uncommon use for this purpose. In this study, we have synthesized a fluorescent polymeric hydrogel, P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) utilizing oligo(ethylene glycol)methyl ether methacrylates (OEGMA) and laboratory-synthesized fluorescent anthrapyrazolone containing monomer, 2-(benzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c’d]bis(indazole)-2(7H)-yl)ethyl methacrylate (DIMA) for the fluorometric detection of TC in the aqueous medium. The P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel exhibited exceptional affinity towards TC while exhibiting high selectivity. The chemosensing behavior of the hydrogel was also compared with the DIMA monomer. Upon adding TC, emission analysis revealed a notable quenching in the emission intensity of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel as well as the DIMA monomer. The Stern Volmer rate constants (Ksv) of the hydrogel (Ksv = 9.99 x 103 M−1) in water and DIMA (Ksv = 25.63 x 103 M−1) in acetonitrile showed a higher quenching rate constant with a limit of detections (LODs) of 1.3 nM and 0.11 nM. Turn-off fluorescence behavior occurs via a static quenching mechanism through a photoinduced electron transfer process and intermolecular hydrogen bonding. To validate the selectivity, the interference analyses in an aqueous medium with different antibiotics are provided. To rationalize the real-time applicability of the hydrogel, studies were extended to water samples obtained from deionized water, tap water, lake water, and also in milk samples. The recovery rate and LOD for real samples ranged from 83.8% to 111% and 0.83 to 3.6 nM, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are a group of antibiotics identified in the 1940s, which effectively hinder protein synthesis by restricting the binding of aminoacyl-tRNA to the ribosomal acceptor site [1,2]. TCs are widely used in the treatment of human and animal infections because of their advantageous antimicrobial properties and low frequency of harmful side effects. Their broad-spectrumactivity extends to uncommon microorganisms such as mycoplasmas, chlamydia, rickettsia, and protozoan parasites in addition to gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [3,4]. However excessive and inappropriate use of antibiotics has increased the amount of TCs in the environment, endangering human health [5]. These traces represent a serious risk since they not only have the potential to be poisonous but also encourage the growth of bacteria that are resistant to drugs [6]. Therefore, it is now necessary to monitor and detect antibiotics, including TCs, as they have become a prominent contaminant class. This need has been answered by the development of several methods, such as capillary electrophoresis analysis [7–9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], electrochemical analysis [12–14], molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [13,15,16], and classical spectrophotometry [17]. Furthermore, chemiluminometry most especially, fluorometry has become more significant because of its excellent accuracy and ease of use [18]. However, it is crucial to note that many fluorometric determinations for TCs reported involve the utilization of transition metals [19–22], their respective metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dots, or composites [20,21,23–30], along with potentially carcinogenic or hazardous compounds [18,31,32]. These kinds of techniques are often required for intricate multistep processes [19,21,23–28,31] and expensive rare-earth elements [33,34]. Despite these difficulties, monitoring and detection of TC antibiotics have come a long way in the last few decades, indicating an increasing demand for efficient analytical methods in this area. Since most TCs are non-fluorescent and their interaction with the fluorophore may be readily observed, it is crucial to use a sensor that produces a fluorescent response to TC.

Fluorescent sensor materials, with excellent sensitivity and fast response time, have been a topic of significant investigation in recent years to develop light-induced chemical sensors for antibiotics with low LOD [33]. Electrostatic interactions, intermolecular hydrogen bonding, and the efficient π-π interaction that exists between the fluorophore and analyte are major factors influencing the binding constants and sensitivity [34,35]. To detect TCs in an aqueous media, Liu et al. have proven the use of the metal–organic framework (MOF) Trans-4,4′-stilbenedicarboxylate Indium (III) (In-sbdc). In-sbdc showed high selectivity for TC, with a LOD of 0.28 − 0.30 μM [36]. Dong et al. designed a fluorescent novel heteroatom-free conjugated tetraphenylethylene polymer (TPE-CMP) with a LOD of 1.23 μg/mL that exhibits fluorescence quenching upon interaction with TC due to photoinduced electron transfer [37]. A dansyl triazole macrocycle (DTMC) was designed by Porel et al. to detect nitro-containing drugs such as dimetridazole (DMI), nitrofurantoin (NFT), and nitrofurazone (NFZ) with extreme selectivity. In the sub-micromolar range, the LOD was found to be extremely low, with DMI having the lowest value (100 nM), followed by NFT (500 nM) and NFZ (1 mM) [38]. Likewise, Huang et. al designed the ([2,3-trimethy-4-[(diethylamino)-2-hydroxy4-yl) vinyl]–3H-benzo[e]indole] (BICL) fluorescent probe for the naked eye detection of citric acid (CA) and acidic pH in real food samples and zebrafish with the LOD of 14 to 23 μM [39]. Yu et al. designed a methyl-4-pyridyl ketone-conjugated pyrene formaldehyde with a p-phenyl boronic acid ester moiety (Pyp-B) for detecting mitochondria H2O2 in living organisms, with a LOD of 0.021 μmol/L [40]. Likewise, a new fluorescent probe with an LOD of 4.6 x 104 CFU/mL was developed from ZnTe quantum dots and modified with mannose for the selective detection of Escherichia coli (E. coli) [41].

Anthrapyrazolone is a significant organic fluorophore molecule renowned for its exceptional properties including high photostability, strong fluorescent emission in the visible region, and high sensitivity in analyte detection [42,43]. These characteristics make anthrapyrazolone an ideal candidate for sensing applications in biological samples or environmental pollutants. They have been widely employed as a synthetic construct in structure and materials design. To detect cyanide and fluoride ions, Prasad et al. investigated the application of a small fluorescent 1,9-pyrazoloanthrone derived compound as a “turn-on” fluorescent probe. This compound has mostly been used as a c-JUN N-terminal kinase (JNK1/2) inhibitor [42]. Similarly, Maji et al. published a study that used (co)polymer and 1,9-pyrazoloanthrone derivatives for the selective detection of trinitrophenol (TNP) with a LOD of 19 ppm [43]. The anthrapyrazolone derivatives were also designed by Maji et al. specifically for the selective detection of thiocyanate and fluoride ions [44].

The preparation of fluorophore-containing polymers for analyte sensing offers enhanced sensitivity and improved signal-to-noise ratio. The incorporation of fluorophores into a polymer matrix enhances stability against photobleaching and chemical degradation and the versatility of polymer enables the customization of functional groups for specific analyte recognition [43]. Furthermore, fluorescence polymers showed more solubility in aqueous medium and common organic solvents as compared to the small fluorophore molecules. Due to their exceptional hydrophilic characteristics, strong biocompatibility, and ability to amplify fluorescence signals, polymer fluorescent sensors have become highly popular in the sensing industry these days. To determine TC in aqueous samples, Zhou et al. combined graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) to develop a unique fluorescence sensor. With a detection limit of about 1.0 µg/L, the GQDs-MIPs exhibited strong selectivity towards the TC [45]. Based on fluorescent coordination polymers [M(2,6-di(carboxyphenyl)pyrazine)] [M (Zn, Mn & Co) (DCPP)], a highly efficient fluorescent chemosensor has been developed for the detection of nitrofurans (NFT and NFZ) and nitroimidazoles (ONZ and MNZ) antibiotics. It offers excellent selectivity and a low LOD [46]. A very sensitive fluorescence sensor was developed by Geng et al. using coordination polymers consisting of mixed ligands [N, N′-bis(4-methylpyridin-4-yl)-2,6-naphthalenedicar boxylamide] and H2CPG [3-(4-chlorophenyl) glutaric acid], which are represented as [M(L)(HCPG)2(H2O)2] (M = Co and M = Ni). This sensor exhibits selective detection of erythromycin (ERY) and oxacillin (OXC) through turn-on behavior, while furaltadone (FTD) can be detected via turn-off behavior. The [M(L)(HCPG)2(H2O)2] (M = Co and M = Ni) demonstrates high concentration ranges with lower LOD [47]. When comparing dye-incorporated polymers with hydrogels, hydrogels possess a wide range of advantages for analyte sensing with highly porous structure, allowing for enhanced diffusion of analytes into the sensing matrix. This increased accessibility to the analyte improves the sensor's responsiveness and reduces response times [48]. Thus, sensing in an aqueous medium is promoted due to the hydrophilic nature of hydrogels where samples are often in liquid form. Moreover, the swelling behavior of hydrogels in response to changes in environmental conditions such as pH, temperature, or solvent composition can be exploited to design stimuli-responsive sensors with tunable sensitivity and selectivity [49,50]. Additionally, drug recovery from aqueous mediums using hydrogels holds significant importance as drug recovery offers a proactive solution by actively removing these substances from the environment. By efficiently trapping and retaining drugs within their network structure, hydrogels reduce the risk of contamination and reduce exposure to pollutants. This approach not only helps preserve water quality but also contributes to the development of sustainable remediation strategies for addressing pharmaceutical pollution, ultimately safeguarding both ecosystem integrity and public health [51,52]. However, researchers have not exploited the application of hydrogels added with fluorescent properties for antibiotic sensing and recovery.

In the present study, anthrapyrazolone-based 2-(benzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c’ d’]bis(indazole)-2(7H)-yl)ethyl methacrylate (DIMA)-functionalized poly(oligoethylene glycol methacrylate) (OEGMA) was used to develop a P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) fluorescent hydrogel via free radical polymerization for TC antibiotic detection. When TC was added, the resulting P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel showed a notable decrease in emission intensity when compared to the DIMA monomer. The Stern-Volmer equation (Ksv) and lifetime measurements were used to study the quenching of fluorescence. Interference studies with different antibiotics were utilized to show selectivity and hydrogel's potential usage was illustrated by its ability to recover TC from a variety of water samples and milk.

Unless otherwise specified, this experiment employed analytical-grade chemicals and solvents that were supplied. The chemicals that were purchased from Sigma Aldrich comprised hydroquinone (HQ), 1,5-dichloroanthraquinone, oligo(ethylene glycol)methyl ether methacrylates (OEGMA), tetracycline (TC), metronidazole (MET), 5-fluoro uracil (5-FU), sulfadiazine (SDZ), sulfamethazine (SMZ), streptomycin (STR), kanamycin (KAN), gentamycin (GEN), and penicillin (PEN) with purity > 98%. TCI Chemicals (India) Pvt. Ltd. was the source of methacrylic anhydride and 2-bromoethanol with > 97% purity. Sodium acetate (CH3COONa) and 2,2′-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were obtained from Lobachemie Pvt. Ltd. India, with purity of 97%. Whereas, 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), and pyridine were obtained from SISCO Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. (SRL), India with 98% purity. AIBN was recrystallized twice from methanol and stored in a freezer. We purchased solvents from Finar Chemicals Ltd. in India, including methanol, dioxane, hexane, triethylamine (TEA), hydrazine monohydrate, dichloromethane (DCM), and N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF). The aforementioned solvents were used for the reaction following distillation. OEGMA monomer was purified by passing through basic alumina immediately before use. As previously reported, 2-((butylthio)carbonothioylthio)propanoic acid was used to synthesize methyl 2-((butylthio)carbonothioylthio)propanoate (CTA-OMe) chain transfer agent [53,54].

Shimadzu's IRTracer-100 FT-IR spectrometer was used to capture Fourier transform infrared spectra utilizing a deuterated lanthanum α-alanine doped triglycine sulphate (DLaTGS) detector. FT-IR for DIMA, P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel was characterized in ATR mode. The measurements were made using an average of 1000 scans at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The Lab Solutions IR analysis software calculated the minimum transmittance values (IR Tracer).

The amount of water that could be retained in the hydrogel network is measured by the equilibrium water content (EWC), which was used to characterize the swelling properties of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. The weight of the hydrogel was measured after it was gently dried with a paper towel after being immersed in water for 24 h at 25 °C (Ws). It was dried, and its weight was recorded again (Wd). Using Eq.
(1) , the EWC of every hydrogel was determined.

The hydrogel was developed following the earlier instructions and allowed to dry at room temperature. Following preparation, the hydrogel was immersed in water. To get rid of unreacted monomer precursors, the water was changed often. The hydrogel was taken out at certain times and gently dried, and its weight was noted. Eq.
(2) , where Wt is the weight of the hydrogel at each time point and W0 is the initial wet hydrogel weight (before swelling), was used to calculate the swelling factor (SF).

An Anton Paar MCR 92 rheometer has been used for rheological experiments. To evaluate the sample, a cone and plate geometry with a 25 mm diameter was employed. We recorded the strain sweep while keeping the temperature constant at 25 °C and varied the strain at a fixed frequency of 1.5 Hz. For the hydrogel sample, the strain has been adjusted from 0.001 to 100% to assess changes in the samples' storage and loss modulus.

The synthesized DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel's UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded in solvents such as acetonitrile (ACN) and water using an Agilent Cary 5000 double-beam UV–Vis absorption spectrometer.

Fluorescence studies were conducted at room temperature using an FLS-1000 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh). The steady-state emission spectra and lifetimes of the DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel were recorded in both ACN and water medium using 380 nm (LED) and 450 nm diode laser (ps). The initial emission spectrum was obtained with excitation at λex = 380 ± 2 nm in a 3.0 mL solution of DIMA (10 μM) and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel (1 mg/mL) in the cuvette. The cuvette was filled with aliquots of various antibiotic solutions (10 µL) in water. The cuvette was closed, the constituents were completely mixed by agitation, and a fluorescence spectrum was obtained after each addition. The same procedure was repeated with varying analyte concentrations to achieve considerable fluorescence quenching and calculate the Stern-Volmer association rate constants (Ksv) to know the sensitivity of DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel to different antibiotics. The well-known Stern Volmer Eq.
(3) was used to plot the relative fluorescence intensity against the anion concentration to get the Ksv.

The quenching efficiency was examined, the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded, and interference investigations involving various analytes were carried out similarly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 2-(benzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c’ d’]bis(indazole)-2(7H)-yl)ethyl methacrylate (DIMA) monomer and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The 2-(benzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c’ d’]bis(indazole)-2(7H)-yl)ethyl methacrylate (DIMA) monomer was synthesized based on the three-step technique described in the literature [44]. Initially, 2,7-dihydrobenzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c'd']bis(indazole) (DI) was synthesized by combining 5.0 g (18 mmol) of 1,5-dichloroanthraquinone and 3.6 g (43 mmol) of sodium acetate with 25 mL of pyridine, to which 6 mL of hydrazine monohydrate was added. The reaction mixture was refluxed for 24 h at 120 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. Following the end of the process, the pyridine was extracted, and the resulting crude substance was purified using column chromatography using a mix of solvents consisting of 70% DCM and 30% EtOAc to obtain pure DI with a yield of 73.9%. Subsequently, 2-(benzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c'd']bis(indazole)-2(7H)-yl)ethanol (DIOH) was obtained by further reacting DI (1.94 g, 8.35 mmol) with 2 mL of 2-bromoethanol in the presence of anhydrous K2CO3 (3.5 g, 25 mmol) using DMF as a solvent, heated at 80 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. The pure DIOH was isolated by column chromatography using ethyl acetate and DCM, resulting in a brownish solid with a 37.5% yield. Finally, DIMA was synthesized by reacting DIOH (0.25 g, 0.78 mmol) with HQ inhibitor (0.13 g, 1.24 mmol) and DMAP catalyst (0.95 g, 7.8 mmol) in DCM. Dropwise additions of 1.09 mmol of TEA were introduced to the reaction solution at 0 °C in a nitrogen environment, and the mixture was stirred. Next, 0.93 mmol of methacrylic anhydride was gradually added to the reaction mixture, which was then stirred for 24 h at 0 °C. Using column chromatography with EtOAc and DCM, the crude product was purified. 1H NMR spectroscopy is given in Fig. S1
, ESI†. 1H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO–d6) 13.23 (s, 1H), 7.55–7.43 (m, 6H), 5.83 (s, 1H), 5.55 (s, 1H), 4.78 (t, 2H), 4.55 (t, 2H), 1.70 (s, 3H).

The synthesis of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel was performed via RAFT polymerization method. OEGMA (1000 mg, 2 mmol) DIMA (34.437 mg, 0.1 mmol), methyl 2-(((butylthio)carbonothioyl)thio)propanoate (CTA-OMe) (5.292 mg, 0.021 mmol), and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) (0.82 mg. 0.000525 mmol) were added into 1.4 mL of dioxane. The reaction mixture was dried under an argon flow. Polymerization was carried out at 75 °C for two hours in an argon atmosphere, resulting in an orangish-red P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. Subsequently, the synthesized hydrogel material was washed two to three times with dioxane. Finally, the resulting hydrogel material was purified via dialysis over a period of two days to remove any residual monomer and other substrates by using distilled water. The molar ratios of reactants were [OEGMA]:[DIMA]:[CTA]:[AIBN] = [95]:[5]:[1]:[0.2], with a monomer concentration of 1.5 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemosensing behavior of compounds with antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of the DIMA compound (1.0 mM) was prepared in an ACN solution. Similarly, a dispersed stock solution of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel (1 mg/mL) was prepared in water. Dilutions were then carried out in a series to enable fluorescence-based detection of the antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
2-(Benzo[1,2,3-cd:4,5,6-c' d']bis(indazole)-2(7H)-yl)ethyl methacrylate (DIMA) monomer was synthesized according to the three-step process described in the literature (Scheme 1 ) [44]. Subsequently, the DIMA-functionalized OEGMA copolymer hydrogel, P(OEGMA-co-DIMA), was synthesized utilizing methyl 2-(((butylthio)carbonothioyl)thio) propanoate (CTA-OMe) via RAFT polymerization technique (Scheme 1). The structural conformation of DIMA was determined by 1H NMR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S1
ESI†. The spectrum showed that the four methylene protons were shown by two triplets at δ 4.78 and 4.55 ppm, and the existence of the methyl proton from the methacrylate unit was indicated by a singlet at δ 1.70 ppm. Two additional protons, suggestive of two vinyl protons attached to the same carbon, also appeared at δ 5.83 and 5.55 ppm. The number of six aromatic protons observed in the range of δ 7.55 to 7.43 ppm. Notably, the –NH proton in the anthrapyrazolone aromatic unit was confirmed as the signal at δ 13.23 ppm. Therefore, the 1H NMR spectrum reveals the successive formation of DIMA monomer. The successive formation of the DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel was confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra of DIMA and the crosslinked P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel are shown in Fig. 1
a. In the DIMA spectrum, –NH and C = O stretching vibrations were noted at 3316 and 1702 cm−1, respectively. After cross-linking, a broad peak appeared around 3420 cm−1 in the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel, indicating the presence of additional hydrogen bonding (–OH) due to the cross-linking of DIMA by OEGMA. Moreover, in the FTIR spectrum of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel, slight shifts were observed in the –NH and C = O stretching vibrations at 2863 and 1721 cm−1, respectively, due to the cross-linking of DIMA within the hydrogel matrix. Additionally, the C-O and C-N stretching vibrations of DIMA at 1286 and 1151 cm−1 were observed, with minor frequency changes in the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel (1253 cm−1 for C-O and 1079 cm−1 for C-N), suggesting the influence of crosslinking on the hydrogel structure. Therefore, based on these FTIR spectral analyses, it can be concluded that the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel was successfully formed. The swelling ability of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel was evaluated using the swelling factor (SF) over time. The excellent swelling factor observed in the designed hydrogel can be attributed to both complete polymerization and efficient crosslinking within its matrix. Fig. 1 b presents a graphical representation of the swelling factor of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel in distilled water over time, demonstrating a consistent qualitative trend. Initially, at time (t) = 0, the SF is 0. Subsequently, a significant increase in SF is observed as the t values increase from 15 min to 120 min. Up to 120 min, an excellent SF was observed for the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel, indicating its efficient swelling property. After 120 min, the hydrogel’s stability starts to decrease due to the weakening of the hydrogen bonding in the hydrogel network. The swelling behavior of water over different time intervals is shown in Fig. 1 c. The capacity of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel to hold water within the hydrogel network (EWC > 59 ± 3.5%) showed that it has good swelling capabilities.

The developed P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel is strong and capable of withstanding high levels of stress without breaking. The stress of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel may attain a strength of 1757 Pa and a strain of 10.29% with an effective stretching ability, as shown in Fig. 1 d.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 e & f, the hydrogel's elastic response was evaluated using its storage modulus (G'), loss modulus (G“), and loss factor (tan δ = G”/G'). Whereas the G'' offers information about the sample's liquid-like (viscous) behavior, the evolution of the G' provides information about the solid-like (elastic) behavior of the sample. P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel, as shown in Fig. 1 e, has a higher G′ than G″, indicating that elasticity rather than viscosity contributes more to the strong hydrogel attribute (G' > G''). In the end, the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel displayed a stable and low loss factor, signifying that the hydrogel had good elastic recovery characteristic properties (Fig. 1 f).

The photophysical properties of the designed DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel were investigated using UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption spectra of both DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel were recorded in ACN and water solvents. In both systems, a broad absorption band at 300 ± 2 nm, attributed to the n → π* transition along with a second absorption maximum at 366 ± 2 nm and 382 ± 2 nm due to the π → π* transition of the anthrapyrazolone moiety is observed (Fig. 2
a). Additionally, strong absorption maxima at 404 ± 2 nm were observed, arising from intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) resulting from the incorporation of the monomeric functional group into the OEGMA polymeric backbone. The emission spectrum of both DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel upon excitation at λmax = 404 nm revealed strong blue emission, around 410 nm for P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel and 427 nm for DIMA, as shown in Fig. 2 b. The wide and strong emission bands detected in both cases are likely due to the π-π* electronic transitions within the DIMA ligand. The electron-rich nature of both the DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel materials, characterized by their high emission, motivated us to investigate potential donor–acceptor interactions with highly electron-deficient antibiotic compounds. This investigation aims to determine their viability for application as chemosensors to detect antibiotics in various water and milk samples.

The investigation of the sensing of several pharmaceuticals is crucial due to their increasing presence as contaminants in water bodies, posing significant health risks to humans and animals. Therefore, it is essential to develop methods or materials for the detection and quantification of these drug molecules across different systems. To address this need, we have selected several commonly used pharmaceutical drugs for human and animal treatments, including antibiotics, antiprotozoal, and anti-inflammatory drugs. The drugs chosen for analysis include Tetracycline (TC), Metronidazole (MET), 5-Fluro Uracil (5-FU), Sulfadiazine (SDZ), Sulfamethazine (SMZ), Streptomycin (STR), Kanamycin (KAN), Gentamycin (GEN) and Penicillin (PEN) as shown in Fig. 3 .

Fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to study the interaction of drugs with DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel, using their high emission properties at 410 and 427 nm, respectively. Notably, significant fluorescence quenching was observed for the drug TC, followed by MET, while other analytes showed minimal changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4
a and
Figs. S2 & S3 ESI† ). Upon gradual addition of TC solution to DIMA (10 μM), a progressive decrease in fluorescence at ∼ 410 nm was observed. Exactly, the addition of 150 μM TC solution resulted in an 88% decrease in emission intensity in ACN solvent (Fig. 4 a), whereas other antibiotics led to only a 13–44% decrease in emission intensity. This observation resulted in further investigation into the potential of these materials for antibiotic detection using DIMA monomer. Similarly, P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel exhibited a 78% decrease in initial fluorescence intensity under similar conditions (Fig. 4 b). Notably, the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel showed a more rapid reduction in fluorescence intensity in the water medium, facilitating real-time sample analysis.

The fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced with the addition of a low concentration of TC and completely quenched at a higher concentration. When 30 μM TC solution was added to P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel, the fluorescence emission was quenched by 22%, however, when 150 μM of TC solution was added, the fluorescence emission was quenched by 78%. Moreover, a 98% reduction in emission intensity was seen upon increasing the concentration of TC solution to 350 μM (Fig. 4 b). The significant color difference in the fluorescence emission of the solutions before and after the addition of TC is displayed in the inset in Fig. 4 a and 4b. These results indicate that P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel exhibited enhanced sensing ability in water.

Furthermore, other antibiotics were also used in the fluorescence titration studies. The differences in emission intensity for various analyte concentrations are compiled in Figs. S2 & S3, ESI† . The sudden reduction in fluorescent intensity observed with an increase in TC concentration indicates efficient photo-induced electron transfer (PET) from the electron-deficient TC to the anthrapyrazolone units on the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. The difference in the quenching behavior upon the addition of various antibiotic concentrations is shown in Fig. 5
a & 5b. The highest selectivity was observed for the TC with P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel and showed high quenching efficiency. The rapid reduction in fluorescent intensity observed with an increase in TC concentration indicates effective PET from the electron-deficient TC to the anthrapyrazolone units on the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. The difference in quenching activity upon the addition of various antibiotic concentrations is shown in Fig. 5 a & 5b. The TC with P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel exhibited the highest quenching efficiency and highest selectivity.

The Stern-Volmer equation was used to measure the sensitivity of fluorescence quenching of DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. Higher sensitivity of the fluorophore to the analyte is indicated by higher Ksv values. The Ksv plot for the change in Io/I in response to the various TC analyte concentrations is shown in Fig. 6
a & 6b. The Io/I ratios in both cases increased linearly as the TC concentration increased, indicating that the static quenching mechanism is the main reason for the quenching of fluorescent intensity. For DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel, the determined Ksv values are found to be 25.63 x 103 and 9.99 x 103 M−1, respectively. Compared to the other antibiotics, TC exhibits higher binding efficiency, evidenced by the greater Ksv value observed for both systems. The calculated Ksv values, which show the order of sensitivity for the various antibiotics, are summarised in Table S1
, ESI†.

The LOD for DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel was calculated by analysing the emission intensities plotted against the concentration of TC ( Fig. S4
, ESI†). The final LODs were calculated using the formula LOD = 3.3xσ/m, where m represents the slope and σ denotes the standard deviation, to establish the signal-to-noise ratio. The LODs for DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel were found to be 0.11 nM and 1.3 nM, respectively.

The selective detection of TC among different analytes motivates us to evaluate its selectivity in the presence of different analytes in the water medium. A sensor system in monitoring real-time samples gains special importance from such a study. The P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel's emission spectrum (1 mg/mL) was first recorded to carry out this competitive fluorescence quenching. To develop an effective interaction, 25 μM of MET solution was added to this solution and allowed to equilibrate for 5 min. After adding MET into the solution, there was no considerable change in the emission maximum. A significant reduction in the fluorescence was observed when emission spectra were recorded after the addition of 25 μM TC to the resultant solution. Emission data was collected after adding the same amounts of TC and MET multiple times. The addition of MET did not provide the considerable emission quenching shown in Fig. 7
a, however, it was observed that the addition of TC showed quenching behavior in all cycles. When other antibiotics were employed instead of MET, similar outcomes were obtained as well ( Fig. S5
, ESI†). Likewise, Fig. 7 b displays the difference in the quenching efficiency of hydrogel upon the addition of various amounts of TC in the presence of other antibiotics. Even in the presence of different antibiotics in the water, the remarkable selectivity of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel towards TC is demonstrated by an effective decrease in quenching efficiency. Research has also been carried out on the more effective detection of TC by the DIMA monomer when compared to other antibiotics (Fig. 7 c & S6, ESI†).

The results of our study indicate that the DIMA fluorescent monomer exhibits slightly higher quenching efficiency, higher Ksv, and a lower LOD for TC compared to the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. Notably, the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel efficiently responds in aqueous media, while DIMA exhibits solubility exclusively in organic media. Furthermore, compared to DIMA, the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel displays favorable hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, efficient sensitivity, efficient visual change, and selectivity towards TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for antibiotics by P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To get more insight to understand the mechanism of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel's fluorescence quenching mechanism in the presence of TC-containing heteroaromatics can be explained via the following studies. ICT mechanism alone explains the fluorescence of the anthrapyrazolone group. Fluorescence quenching was observed and the ICT decreased with the addition of an electron-deficient heteroaromatic TC compound. The pyrazole in the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel can help in bringing the analyte to its vicinity to establish a π-π interaction. The –NH group in the hydrogel can also make a hydrogen bond with the TC. This combined effect can make the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel a good candidate for sensing antibiotics.

The excitation spectra of TC and the emission and excitation spectra of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel are shown in Fig. 8
a & S7, ESI†. Strong absorption bands were seen in the TC at 275 and 356 nm, which overlaps with the excitation spectra of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. As a result, it suggests that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching for TC could be facilitated by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or the inner filter effect (IFE) [55]. We measured the fluorescence lifetimes of the DIMA ( Fig. S8
, ESI†) and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogels (Fig. 8 b) both before and after varying the concentration of TC to verify this procedure. There is not much variation in the lifetime values and fluorescence lifetime decay pattern. This indicates that static quenching phenomena is responsible for the quenching process. According to the mechanism via Ksv plot (Fig. 8 c), the change in Io/I with respect to TC concentration is linear, suggesting that the addition of TC leads the fluorescence to be statically quenched. It further demonstrates that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was IFE rather than FRET, as fluorescence lifetime exhibits an insignificant change in fluorescence lifetime as characteristic of IFE mechanism [56]. The fluorescence internal filtration effect is usually a result of the absorption of excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores by light-absorbing compounds, which causes a decrease or complete quenching of fluorescence intensity [57,58].

Additionally, the TC can also interact with the imine and tertiary amine in the anthrapyrazolone unit of the DIMA and P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. As a result, the rapid electrostatic interactions between the fluorophore molecules and their lone-pair nitrogen atoms will accelerate the process of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) resulting in efficient fluorescence quenching. We have correlated the emission spectrum of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel (donor) with the absorption spectra of the antibiotics (acceptor) to gain a deeper knowledge of the quenching process (Fig. 8 a). The electron transfer process is an important phenomenon for the quenching process, which is demonstrated by an absence of important spectrum overlap between the emission spectra of the donor (P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel) unit and acceptor (TC) absorption. This experimental evidence reveals that the high quenching rate of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel with TC is facilitated by a strong ground state charge transfer complex formed by ion pair electrostatic interactions along with a primary PET process and a static quenching pathway. The fluorescent behavior of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel is primarily attributed to the DIMA monomer. The PET process between DIMA and TC potentially leads to the quenching of the fluorescence of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel. Specifically, electrons from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) of DIMA are excited to its Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO), followed by transfer to the LUMO of TC. To investigate this process, we employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the B3LYP/6-31G* level to determine the relative energies of the HOMOs and LUMOs of DIMA and TC, as shown in Fig. 8 d. The resulting energy band gap between the HOMOs and LUMOs of DIMA is 3.50 eV. Notably, the LUMO energy level of DIMA (−1.73 eV) is higher than that of TC (−2.11 eV), indicating support for the PET from DIMA to TC [59]. Thus, it is concluded that both the PET process and the strong absorption of TC at the excitation wavelength (404 nm) contribute to the fluorescence quenching of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel in determining TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practicability of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel for the detection of TC, the studies were extended to real samples. To achieve this, a fluorescence P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel sensor was constructed and employed for TC detection in various samples including milk and different water sources. Consequently, both the standard addition method and the recovery test method were utilized. The obtained results, as detailed in Table 1 , demonstrated a recovery rate ranging from 83.8% to 111%. These findings underscore the excellent repeatability and precision of the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel sensing system and signify there is no interference from the other contaminants in tap water, lake water, and the milk sample as we observed the similar recovery (%) as that of deionized water, affirming its utility for TC detection in both water and milk samples. All the samples demonstrated LODs in the 0.83–3.6 nM range.

In this work, we have designed the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel and its application as a fluorescence-based material towards various antibiotics has been successfully demonstrated. Fluorescence studies displayed the excellent selectivity and sensitivity for TC towards P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel and DIMA in the presence of competitive other antibiotics. The results of our study indicate that the DIMA fluorescent monomer exhibits slightly higher quenching efficiency, higher Stern Volmer rate constant (Ksv = 25.63 x 103 M−1), and a lower LOD (0.11 nM) for TC compared to the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel (Ksv = 25.63 x 103 M−1), LOD = 1.3 nM. Notably, the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel demonstrates an efficient response in aqueous media, while DIMA exhibits solubility exclusively in organic media. Furthermore, in comparison to DIMA, the P(OEGMA-co-DIMA) hydrogel displays favorable hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, and efficient sensitivity and selectivity towards TC. A static quenching mechanism with an intermolecular hydrogen bonding and photoinduced electron transfer process induces turn-off fluorescence behavior. The interference studies with other antibiotics in a water medium were discussed to validate the selectivity. Finally, we demonstrated its potential application using different water and milk samples.

Venkatramaiah Nutalapati: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Samarendra Maji: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
